zh.lifeSummary: 當Sir Thomas Alfred Yeard Knight of Denton Court於1487年出生在英國薩默塞特時，他的父親Sir Thomas Alfred Yeard Knight of Denton Court22歲，母親Elizabeth Margaret Banfield17歲。他於1513年與Lady Joan Scotts結婚。他們至少是1個兒子和1個女兒的父母。他去世於1557年10月23日在英國戴文牛頓 阿博特享年70歲，埋葬在英國英格蘭戴文牛頓 阿博特。

nameMeanings.name: Yeard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (Devon): perhaps a variant of Peart with voicing of final -d.

variants.name: Peart
meanings: Scottish: habitational name from Perth (Perths), which is recorded as Pertc. 1128. The placename derives from Pictish perta ‘(at the) thicket, copse’.
English: nickname, probably from a shortened form of Middle English, Old French apert ‘open, plain to see’, of persons ‘frank, honest’ or ‘accomplished’.

nameMeanings.name: Knight
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: status or occupational name from Middle English knight ‘retainer, attendant’ (Old English cniht ‘boy, youth, lad)’. The specialized feudal sense ‘a high-ranking tenant bound to serve his lord as a mounted soldier’ is not known to have ever given rise to the surname, although it is not out of the question that it may occasionally have been used as a nickname, perhaps for someone who played the part of an armed knight in a local pageant.
Irish: sometimes adopted for Gaelic Mac an Ridire ‘son of the knight’. See also McKnight .

variants.name: Knights
meanings: English (East Anglia): variant of Knight , with post-medieval excrescent -s.

variants.name: McKnight
meanings: Irish: part translation of Gaelic Mac an Ridire ‘son of the horseman (Gaelic ridire)’, formerly Anglicized McRuddery.
Scottish (Dumfriesshire and Galloway) and Irish (Ulster): Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Neachtain, which is usually Anglicized as McNaughton .

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese: Cheng, Jing, Kam Fung, Wai Shing, Wing, Yali, Yuk.
Chinese:
variant Romanization of the surnames 歐 and 區, see   Ou   1 and 2.

variants.name: Ou
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Wei, Ying, Ching, Heng, Hong, Jia, Kuang Yu, Li-Ching, Cho, Chong, Chung, Sa.  Southeast Asian Lichun, Ming, Quan, Roeun, Sokhom, Chau, Hai, Leang, Ly, Nhanh, Phan, Pho, Tha.

Chinese: Mandarin form of the surname 歐, meaning ‘to sing someone's praise’ in ancient Chinese: (i) from the placename Dong Ou (東甌), a fief (in present-day Wenzhou in Zhejiang province) which was granted to Yao in 192  BC . It is said that Yao was a descendant of Wujiang (reigned 355–333  BC ), king of the state of Yue (located mainly in present-day Zhejiang and Jiangxi provinces) during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476  BC ). (ii) from the placename Ou Yu (歐餘), the name of a fief (located in present-day Zhejiang province) that was granted to Ti, the second son of Wujiang (reigned 355–333  BC ), king of the state of Yue (located mainly in present-day Zhejiang and Jiangxi provinces) during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476  BC ).

Chinese: Mandarin form of the surname 區, originally denoting an ancient vessel for measurement in ancient Chinese: from the personal name of Ou Zhizi (歐治子), an expert sword-maker who lived during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476 BC ). Some of his descendants inherited Ou (歐) as their surname, simplifying the Chinese character 歐 to 區.

nameMeanings.name: Denton
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: habitational name from any of numerous places so called. The vast majority, including those in Cambridgeshire, Cumbria, Dumfries, Durham, Kent, Lancashire, Lincolnshire, Norfolk, Northumberland, Oxfordshire, Sussex, and Yorkshire are named from Old English denu ‘valley’ (see Dean 1) + tūn ‘enclosure, settlement’. An isolated example in Northamptonshire appears in the Domesday Book as Dodintone meaning ‘enclosure, settlement associated with Dodda or Dudda’.

variants.name: Dean
meanings: English: topographic name from Middle English dene ‘valley’ (Old English denu), or a habitational name from any of several places in various parts of England named Dean or Dene from this word.

English: nickname or occupational name for the servant of a dean or nickname for someone thought to resemble a dean. A dean was an ecclesiastical official, the head of a chapter of canons or a church official with jurisdiction over a sub-division of an archdeaconry. Though no doubt some deans had illegitimate children, they were officially celibate, and in the main the surname is probably a nickname in origin, similar to Bishop , Prior , Priest , and Monk . The Middle English word deen, dien, dein, is a borrowing of Old French d(e)ien, doien from Latin decanus (originally a leader of ten men, from decem ‘ten’), and thus is a cognate of Deacon .
English: from the Middle English personal name Deyne (or Dene) a rhyming pet form of Reynald (see Reginald ).

nameMeanings.name: Court
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: topographic or occupational name from Middle English curt, court ‘large house’ (a word of the same ultimately Latin origin as 2 below), denoting someone who lived or worked in a manor house or castle, a courtly retainer.
French: topographic name from Old French co(u)rt ‘court, farmyard’ (from Latin cohors, cors, genitive cohortis, cortis ‘court, farmyard, enclosure’).

English and French: nickname for a physically small man, or ironically for a large man, from the adjective Middle English curt, Old French co(u)rt ‘short, small’ (from Latin curtus ‘curtailed, truncated, cut short, broken off’). Compare French Lecourt .

variants.name: Courtade
meanings: French (mainly southern): topographic name derived from Old French co(u)rt ‘court, farmyard’ (see Court ), or a habitational name from (La) Courtade, the name of several places in the southwestern part of France.

variants.name: Cournoyer
meanings: French Canadian: topographic name for someone living at a farm with a walnut tree in the farmyard, from French cour(t) ‘court, farmyard’ (see Court ) + noyer ‘walnut tree’. Compare Courneya .

History: This surname was originally a secondary surname or dit (‘also called’) name, borne Pierre Hus dit Cournoyer from QC (a son of Paul Hus from Montigny in Seine-Maritime, France; see Hus ), who married Jeanne Vanet in QC in 1700. His descendants also bear altered forms of the former secondary surname, such as Courneya, and also the surname Paulette .

variants.name: Curt
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Dieter, Frieda.
English (Lincolnshire): variant of   Court  .
Americanized form of German   Kurt  .

variants.name: Curtis
meanings: English: nickname for a refined person, sometimes perhaps given ironically, from Middle English, Old French courteis, courtois, curtis ‘courtly, refined, urbane’ (derivative of Old French court; see Court 1).

variants.name: Lecourt
meanings: French: nickname for a physically small man (or ironically for a large man), a variant of Court , with fused masculine definite article le.

variants.name: Courtemanche
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Emile, Amedee, Andre, Armand, Fernand, Herve, Lucien, Marcel, Normand, Ovide, Raoul.

French: habitational name from Courtemanche or Courdemanche, the name of several places in Somme, Sarthe, Eure, and Orne, derived from Latin cortis dominica ‘lord's farm or estate’ (see Court ). Compare Shortsleeve .
History: Antoine Courtemanche dit Jolicoeur from Bannes in Sarthe, France, married Élisabeth Haquin in Montreal, QC, in 1663.

variants.name: Courtier
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Patrice.
French: habitational name from any of the places called Courtier (Seine-et-Marne, Alpes-de-Haute-Provence) or Courtière (Loir-et-Cher).
French: occupational name or perhaps a nickname from Old French courtier ‘arbitrator, judge’.

variants.name: Cort
meanings: Catalan: from cort ‘court’ (from Latin cohors, cors, genitive cohortis, cortis ‘court, farmyard, enclosure’), an occupational name for someone who worked in a manorial court or a topographic name for someone who lived in or by one.
English: variant of   Court  .
Americanized form of Dutch, German, and Jewish   Kort  .

variants.name: Courchesne
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Armand, Aime, Andre, Aurel, Emile, Gaston, Germain, Gilles, Jean-Claude, Marcel, Marcelle.

French Canadian: topographic name for someone living at a farm with an oak tree in the farmyard, from (Old) French cour(t) ‘court, farmyard’ (see Court ) + Old French chesne (French chêne) ‘oak’. Compare Courchaine .

variants.name: Courts
meanings: English: variant of Court , with genitival or post-medieval excrescent -s.
Americanized form of German Kurtz or its Dutch cognate Koerts.

nameMeanings.name: Sir
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: equivalent of George .

variants.name: George
meanings: Via Old French and Latin, from Greek Georgios (a derivative of geōrgos ‘farmer’, from gē ‘earth’ + ergein ‘to work’). This was the name of several early saints, including the shadowy figure who is now the patron of England (as well as of Germany and Portugal). If the saint existed at all, he was perhaps martyred in Palestine in the persecutions of Christians instigated by the Emperor Diocletian at the beginning of the 4th century. The popular legend in which the hero slays a dragon is a medieval Italian invention. He was for a long time a more important saint in the Orthodox Church than in the West, and the name was not much used in England during the Middle Ages, even after St George came to be regarded as the patron of England in the 14th century. Its use increased from the 1400s, and by 1500 it was regularly among the most popular male names. This popularity was reinforced when George I came to the throne in 1714 , bringing this name with him from Germany. It has been one of the most popular English boys' names ever since.

nameMeanings.name: Thomas
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: New Testament name, borne by one of Christ's twelve apostles, referred to as ‘Thomas, called Didymus’ (John 11:16; 20:24). Didymos is the Greek word for ‘twin’, and the name is the Greek form of an Aramaic byname meaning ‘twin’. The given name has always been popular throughout Christendom, in part because St Thomas's doubts have made him seem a very human character.

nameMeanings.name: Alfred
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From an Old English name derived from ælf ‘elf, supernatural being’ + ræd ‘counsel’. It was a relatively common name before the Norman Conquest of Britain, being borne most notably by Alfred the Great ( 849–899 ), King of Wessex. After the Conquest it was adopted by the Normans in a variety of more or less radically altered forms ( see Avery ). In some regions the forms Alvery and Avery never fell entirely out of favour and became locally popular in the 16th century. It provides a rare example ( Edward is another) of a distinctively Old English name that has spread widely on the Continent. It was strongly revived in the 19th century, along with other names of pre-Conquest historical figures, faded in the mid-20th century, but has since recovered some popularity.

historicEvents.title: Book of Common Prayer
historicEvents.description: Book of Common Prayer, a product of the English Reformation, was published in 1549 for assistance in the administration of the sacraments and other rites and ceremonies of the church according to the use of the Church of England. The book outlined morning, evening, and communion prayers and orders for baptism and marriage, thus making England a truly Protestant state.

en.lifeSummary: When Sir Thomas Alfred Yeard Knight of Denton Court was born in 1487, in Somerset, England, his father, Sir Thomas Alfred Yeard Knight of Denton Court, was 22 and his mother, Elizabeth Margaret Banfield, was 17. He married Lady Joan Scotts in 1513. They were the parents of at least 1 son and 1 daughter. He died on 23 October 1557, in Newton Abbot, Devon, England, at the age of 70, and was buried in Newton Abbot, Devon, England, United Kingdom.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Sir Thomas Alfred Yeard Knight of Denton Court est né en 1487, à Somerset, Angleterre, son père, Sir Thomas Alfred Yeard Knight of Denton Court, avait 22 ans et sa mère, Elizabeth Margaret Banfield, avait 17 ans. Il a épousé Lady Joan Scotts en 1513. Ils ont eu au moins 1 fils et 1 fille. Il est décédé le 23 octobre 1557, à Newton Abbot, Devon, Angleterre, à l’âge de 70 ans, et a été enterré à Newton Abbot, Devon, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni.

Er hat 1513 Lady Joan Scotts geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 1 Sohn und 1 Tochter. Er starb am 23. Oktober 1557 im Alter von 70 Jahren in Newton Abbot, Devon, England und wurde in Newton Abbot, Devon, England, United Kingdom begraben.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Sir Thomas Alfred Yeard Knight of Denton Court è nato il nel 1487 nel Somerset, England, il suo padre, Sir Thomas Alfred Yeard Knight of Denton Court, aveva 22 anni e il suo madre, Elizabeth Margaret Banfield, aveva 17 anni. Lui sposato Lady Joan Scotts nel 1513. Erano genitori di almeno 1 figlio e 1 figlia. Morì il 23 ottobre 1557, nel Newton Abbot, Devon, England, all'età di 70 anni, e fu sepolto a Newton Abbot, Devon, Inghilterra, Regno Unito.

ja.lifeSummary: Sir Thomas Alfred Yeard Knight of Denton Courtが1487年にイギリスソメルセトで生まれた時，父Sir Thomas Alfred Yeard Knight of Denton Courtは22歳，母Elizabeth Margaret Banfieldは17歳であった。1513年にLady Joan Scottsと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも1人の息子と1人の娘の親であった。彼は1557年10月23日にイギリスデボンニュートン アボットで70歳で死去し，イギリスイギリスデボンニュートン アボットにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: Sir Thomas Alfred Yeard Knight of Denton Court(이)가 1487년 영국 서머세트에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Sir Thomas Alfred Yeard Knight of Denton Court(은)는 22세였고, 그의 어머니 Elizabeth Margaret Banfield(은)는 17세였다. 그는 1513년 Lady Joan Scotts(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 한 명의 아들과 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1557년 10월 23일 영국 데폰 뉴턴 애벗에서 70세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 잉글랜드 데폰 뉴턴 애벗에 묻혔다.

pt.lifeSummary: Quando Sir Thomas Alfred Yeard Knight of Denton Court nasceu em 1487, em Somerset, Inglaterra, seu pai, Sir Thomas Alfred Yeard Knight of Denton Court, tinha 22 anos e sua mãe, Elizabeth Margaret Banfield, tinha 17 anos. Ele casou-se com Lady Joan Scotts em 1513. Eles tiveram pelo menos 1 filho e 1 filha. Ele faleceu em 23 de outubro de 1557, em Newton Abbot, Devon, Inglaterra, com 70 anos, e foi sepultado em Newton Abbot, Devon, Inglaterra, United Kingdom.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Sir Thomas Alfred Yeard Knight of Denton Court родился в 1487 г. в местности сомирсет, Англия, его отцу по имени Sir Thomas Alfred Yeard Knight of Denton Court было 22 года, а матери, которую звали Elizabeth Margaret Banfield, 17. Он женился на женщине по имени Lady Joan Scotts в 1513 г. У них был по крайней мере 1 сын и 1 дочь. Он умер 23 октября 1557 г. в местности нютон эббот, девон, Англия в возрасте 70 лет и был похоронен в местности нютон эббот, девон, Англия, Великобритания.

es.lifeSummary: Cuando Sir Thomas Alfred Yeard Knight of Denton Court nació en 1487, en Somerset, Inglaterra, su papá, Sir Thomas Alfred Yeard Knight of Denton Court, tenía 22 años y su mamá, Elizabeth Margaret Banfield, tenía 17 años. Se casó con Lady Joan Scotts en 1513. Fueron padres de por lo menos 1 hijo y 1 hija. Murió el 23 de octubre de 1557, en Newton Abbot, Devon, Inglaterra, a la edad de 70 años, y fue sepultado en Newton Abbot, Devon, Inglaterra, Reino Unido.
